#1 · record_id=18 · paper_id=1810.04864v1
Sequence-to-Sequence Models for Data-to-Text Natural Language Generation: Word- vs. Character-based Processing and Output Diversity
Missing: 1Contradicted: 2raw 3verified 3source retry

Caption

Table 4: E2E and WebNLG development set results in the format avg±SD. Human results are averaged over using each human reference as prediction once.

Gold Description

Table 4 shows the BLEU and ROUGE-L development set scores when treating each human reference as prediction once and evaluating it against the remaining references, compared to the scores of the word-based and character-based models Strikingly, on the E2E development set, both model variants significantly outperform human texts by far with respect to both automatic evaluation measures. While the human BLEU score is significantly higher than those of both systems on the WebNLG development set, there is no statistical difference between human and system ROUGE-L scores.

High-level questions
[
  {
    "id": "h1",
    "question": "Which model (word or char) achieves the highest score for each metric, and which achieves the lowest?",
    "rationale": "Identifying maxima and minima per metric reveals the relative strengths of word-based and character-based models across different evaluation measures.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "row_extrema"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h2",
    "question": "How does the performance of word-based and character-based models differ from human performance for each metric?",
    "rationale": "Comparing model scores to human baselines quantifies the gap to human-level generation quality.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "row_comparison"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h3",
    "question": "Is there a correlation between the BLEU and ROUGE-L scores for the two models?",
    "rationale": "Examining correlation helps understand if higher BLEU consistently implies higher ROUGE-L across models.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "column_correlation"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h4",
    "question": "For how many metrics do word or char models surpass human scores?",
    "rationale": "Counting thresholds (human scores) provides a summary of model competitiveness.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "threshold_count"
    ]
  }
]

#2 · record_id=24 · paper_id=1907.13337v1
Simple Unsupervised Summarization by Contextual Matching
Missing: 4raw 4verified 4source base

Caption

Table 3: Comparison of different model choices. The top section evaluates the effects of contextual representation in the matching model, and the bottom section evaluates the effects of different smoothing methods in the fluency model.

Gold Description

Table 3 considers analysis of different aspects of the model. First, we look at the fluency model and compare the cluster smoothing (CS) approach with softmax temperature (TEMPx with x being the temperature) commonly used for generation in LM-integrated models (Chorowski and Jaitly, 2016) as well as no adjustment (NA). Second, we vary the 3-layer representation out of ELMo forward language model to do contextual matching (bot/mid/top: bottom/middle/top layer only, avg: average of 3 layers, cat: concatenation of all layers). Results show the effectiveness of our cluster smoothing method for the vocabulary adaptive language model pfm, although temperature smoothing is an option for abstractive datasets. When using word embeddings (bottom layer only from ELMo language model) in our contextual matching model pcm, the summarization performance drops significantly to below simple baselines as demonstrated by score decrease.

#3 · record_id=33 · paper_id=1901.02534v1
Team Papelo: Transformer Networks at FEVER
Partially covered: 1Missing: 2raw 3verified 3source base

Caption

Table 1: Effect of adding titles to premises.

Gold Description

On a support basis, we find a 52% increase in Kappa by adding the titles. Cohen's Kappa is 68% higher than that for ESIM.

#4 · record_id=50 · paper_id=1908.05267v2
Towards Debiasing Fact Verification Models
Partially covered: 2Missing: 2raw 4verified 4source base

Caption

Table 7: Top 10 LMI-ranked bigrams in the train set of FEVER for Support.

Gold Description

Table 7 and Table 8 summarize the top 10 bigrams for SUPPORT and NOT ENOUGH INFO. The correlation between the biased phrases in the two dataset splits is not as strong as in the REFUTE label, presented in the paper. However, one can notice that some of the biased bigrams in the training set, such as "least one" and "starred movie", translate to cues that can help in predictions over the development set.

#5 · record_id=51 · paper_id=1706.09789v3
Two-Stage Synthesis Networks for Transfer Learning in Machine Comprehension
Covered: 3raw 3verified 3source base

Caption

Table 3: NewsQA to SQuAD. Exact match (EM) and span F1 results on SQuAD development set of a NewsQA BIDAF model baseline vs. one finetuned on SQuAD using the data generated by a 2-stage SynNet (Snet).

Gold Description

report brief results on SQuAD (Table 3), We also evaluate the SynNet on the NewsQAto-SQuAD direction. We directly apply the best setting from the other direction and report the result in Table 3. The SynNet improves over the baseline by 1.6% in EM and 0.7% in F1. Limited by space, we leave out ablation studies in this direction.

      "column_extrema"
    ]
  },

    ]
  },

    ]
  }
]

UdS Submission for the WMT 19 Automatic Post-Editing Task
Partially covered: 3raw 3verified 3source base

Caption

Table 1: BLEU Scores of Data Sets

Gold Description

We then tested the BLEU scores between machine translation results and corresponding gold standard post-editing results on the original development set, the training set and the synthetic data as shown in Table 1. Table 1 shows that there is a significant gap between the synthetic eScape data set (Negri et al., 2018) and the real-life data sets (the development set and the original training set from posteditors),

#7 · record_id=106 · paper_id=1710.03430v3
Learning to Rank Question-Answer Pairs using Hierarchical Recurrent Encoder with Latent Topic Clustering
Partially covered: 2Missing: 1raw 3verified 3source base

Caption

Table 3: Model performance results for the Ubuntu-v1 dataset. Models [1-4] are from Lowe et al. (2015); Kadlec et al. (2015); Wang and Jiang (2016); Wang et al. (2017), respectively.

Gold Description

As Table 3 shows, our proposed HRDE and HRDE-LTC models achieve the best performance for the Ubuntu-v1 dataset. We also find that the RDE-LTC model shows improvements from the baseline model, RDE.

#8 · record_id=109 · paper_id=1910.13794v1
Let Me Know What to Ask: Interrogative-Word-Aware Question Generation
Partially covered: 1Missing: 1raw 2verified 2source retry

Caption

Table 2: Comparison of our model with the baselines. “*” is our QG module.

Gold Description

Our model outperforms all other models in all the metrics. This improvement is consistent, around 2%.

High-level questions
[
  {
    "id": "h1",
    "question": "Which model achieves the highest BLEU-4 and METEOR scores?",
    "rationale": "Identifying the top-performing model on BLEU-4 and METEOR highlights the best overall generation quality.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "column_extrema"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h2",
    "question": "How does IWAQG compare to the best baseline (Liu et al. 2019) in terms of ROUGE-L?",
    "rationale": "Directly comparing IWAQG to the strongest baseline reveals the improvement margin in an important metric.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "row_comparison"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h3",
    "question": "What are the BLEU-1 scores for each model, and which model has the highest?",
    "rationale": "BLEU-1 measures unigram overlap; listing all scores and identifying the max shows the range and top performer.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "column_extrema"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h4",
    "question": "How many models have a BLEU-1 score above 46?",
    "rationale": "Counting models above a threshold gives a sense of overall high performance on BLEU-1.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "threshold_count"
    ]
  }
]

Matched text: Our model outperforms all other models in all the metrics.
Matching reason: The gold description states that our model outperforms all others in all metrics. The candidate insight claims IWAQG is highest on BLEU-1, BLEU-4, and METEOR, and outperforms Liu et al. on ROUGE-L. However, it does not claim highest on ROUGE-L (though data suggests it is), and it omits BLEU-2 and BLEU-3. Thus, the candidate only partially covers the gold statement.

Let Me Know What to Ask: Interrogative-Word-Aware Question Generation

Caption

Table 7: Recall and precision of interrogative words of our interrogative-word classifier.

Gold Description

In addition, we provide the recall and precision per class for our final interrogative-word classifier (CLS + AT + NER in Table 7). However, the recall of which is very low. Our model has also problem with why Lastly, the recall of 'when is also low

High-level questions

  {
    "id": "h1",

    "suggested_operations": [
      "column_extrema"
    ]
  },

    "id": "h2",

    "suggested_operations": [
      "column_extrema"
    ]
  },

    "id": "h3",
    "question": "Is there a significant gap between recall and precision for any class?",

      "row_comparison",
      "threshold_count"
    ]
  },

    "id": "h4",
    "question": "How does precision vary across classes as recall changes?",
    "rationale": "Exploring the correlation between recall and precision provides insight into the overall trade-off in classifier performance.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "column_correlation"
    ]
  }
]

#10 · record_id=122 · paper_id=1707.06961v1
Mimicking Word Embeddings using Subword RNNs
Missing: 1raw 1verified 1source retry

Caption

Table 3: Similarity results on the RareWord set, measured as Spearman’s ρ×100. VarEmbed was trained on a 20-million token dataset, Polyglot on a 1.7B-token dataset.

Gold Description

The results, shown in Table 3, demonstrate that the MIMICK RNN recovers about half of the loss in performance incurred by the original Polyglot training model due to out-of-vocabulary words in the "All pairs" condition. MIMICK also outperforms VarEmbed. FastText can be considered an upper bound: with a vocabulary that is 25 times larger than the other models, it was missing words from only 44 pairs on this data.

High-level questions
[
  {
    "id": "h1",
    "question": "Which embedding method achieves the highest overall similarity score on all pairs?",
    "rationale": "Identifying the top-performing method on the 'All / pairs' metric provides a direct comparison of overall quality across methods.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "column_extrema"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h2",
    "question": "What is the relationship between vocabulary size and overall similarity (All / pairs)?",
    "rationale": "Understanding how vocabulary size correlates with performance can guide resource allocation and model selection.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "column_correlation"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h3",
    "question": "For which method is the difference between in-vocabulary and all-pairs similarity largest?",
    "rationale": "A large gap may indicate overfitting to known words or poor generalization to rare words.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "row_comparison",
      "column_extrema"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h4",
    "question": "How does Polyglot performance change when combined with mimick?",
    "rationale": "Comparing Polyglot#2 and +mimick (same settings) against standalone Polyglot reveals the additive benefit of the mimick mechanism.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "row_comparison"
    ]
  }
]
Low-level questions

    },

  }
]

#11 · record_id=128 · paper_id=1805.10254v1
Neural Argument Generation Augmented with Externally Retrieved Evidence
Covered: 1Missing: 3raw 4verified 4source retry

Caption

Table 4: Evaluation on topic relevance—models that generate arguments highly related with OP should be ranked high by a separately trained relevance estimation model, i.e., higher Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR) and Precision at 1 (P@1) scores. All models trained with evidence significantly outperform seq2seq trained without evidence (approximation randomization testing, p<0.0001).

Gold Description

Ranking metrics of MRR and Precision at 1 (P@1) are utilized, with results reported in Table 4. The ranker yields significantly better scores over arguments generated from models trained with evidence, compared to arguments generated by SEQ2SEQ model.

High-level questions
[
  {
    "id": "h1",
    "question": "Which model achieves the highest MRR and P@1 scores for the Standard Decoder?",
    "rationale": "Identify the top-performing model among standard decoder variants to assess baseline effectiveness.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "column_extrema",
      "row_comparison"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h2",
    "question": "Which model achieves the highest MRR and P@1 scores for the Our Decoder?",
    "rationale": "Identify the top-performing model among our decoder variants, noting that Retrieval has null values.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "column_extrema",
      "row_comparison"
    ]
  },

    "id": "h3",
    "question": "How do models trained with evidence compare to the seq2seq baseline in terms of MRR and P@1?",

      "row_comparison",
      "threshold_count"
    ]

    "id": "h4",

    "rationale": "Check if models that perform well on the standard decoder also perform well on our decoder, ignoring null rows.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "column_correlation"
    ]
  }
]

    "value": "89.76"
  }
]

#12 · record_id=156 · paper_id=1904.00132v2
ANA at SemEval-2019 Task 3: Contextual Emotion detection in Conversations through hierarchical LSTMs and BERT
Missing: 3raw 4verified 3source retry

Caption

Table 2: Label distribution of train, dev, and test set

Gold Description

According to the description in (CodaLab, 2019), the label distribution for dev and test sets are roughly 4% for each of the emotions. However, from the dev set (Table 2) we know that the proportions of each of the emotion categories are better described as %5 each, thereby we use %5 as the empirical estimation of distribution Pte(xtr i ). We did not use the exact proportion of dev set as the estimation to prevent the overfitting towards dev set. The sample distribution of the train set is used as Ptr(xtr i ).

High-level questions
[
  {
    "id": "h1",
    "question": "Which emotion category has the highest and lowest percentage in each dataset (train, dev, test)?",
    "rationale": "Identifying the most and least frequent emotions in each split reveals potential class imbalance and guides focus for model training or evaluation.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "column_extrema"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h2",
    "question": "How does the proportion of the 'others' category change from the training set to the test set?",
    "rationale": "A significant shift in the dominant 'others' class implies dataset distribution shift, which could affect model generalization.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "row_comparison",
      "trend"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h3",
    "question": "What is the size difference between the largest and smallest datasets?",
    "rationale": "Understanding dataset sizes helps assess statistical reliability and potential overfitting or underfitting.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "column_extrema",
      "row_comparison"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h4",
    "question": "What is the combined percentage of the specific emotion categories (happy, angry, sad) in the test set?",
    "rationale": "Summing these percentages quantifies the effective class balance for the targeted emotions in the test set.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "threshold_count"
    ]
  }
]

#13 · record_id=173 · paper_id=1906.04772v1
A Systematic Comparison of English Noun Compound Representations
Missing: 6raw 6verified 6source base

Caption

Table 3: The performance of the best setting for each property.

Gold Description

Table 3 displays the best performing DSMfor each property. There is a preference to word2vec and to a higher embedding dimension.

High-level questions
[
  {
    "id": "h1",
    "question": "Which property achieved the highest F1 score, and what representation and hyperparameters were used?",
    "rationale": "Identifying the best overall performance helps understand which noun compound relation is most effectively modeled and what configuration yields it.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "column_extrema",
      "row_comparison"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h2",
    "question": "Which property exhibits the largest gap between precision and recall, and what does this indicate about the model's behavior?",

    "suggested_operations": [
      "row_comparison",
      "column_extrema"
    ]
  },

    "id": "h3",
    "question": "How does the average F1 score compare across the three representation methods (Co-occurrence, Backtranslation, Matrix)?",
    "rationale": "Comparing representation methods reveals which technique is generally more effective for the noun compound task.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "row_comparison",
      "column_extrema"
    ]
  },

    "id": "h4",

    "suggested_operations": [
      "threshold_count"
    ]
  }
]

#14 · record_id=178 · paper_id=1905.13126v1
Automatic Evaluation of Local Topic Quality
Partially covered: 1Missing: 4raw 5verified 5source base

Caption

Table 2: Coefficient of determination (r2) between global metrics and crowdsourced topic-word matching annotations.

Gold Description

As seen in Table 2, we report the coefficient of determination (r2) for each global metric and dataset. Note that global metrics do correlate somewhat with human judgment of local topic quality. However, the correlation is moderate to poor, especially in the case of coherence

High-level questions
[
  {
    "id": "h1",
    "question": "Which global metric (SigUni or Coherence) achieves the highest r2 values across all datasets?",
    "rationale": "Identify the best-performing metric for topic-word matching across different text corpora.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "row_extrema",
      "row_comparison"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h2",
    "question": "On which dataset does the Coherence metric have the lowest r2, and what is that value?",
    "rationale": "Find the worst-case performance for one metric to understand dataset sensitivity.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "column_extrema",
      "row_extrema"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h3",
    "question": "Is there a consistent performance gap between SigUni and Coherence across all three datasets?",
    "rationale": "Assess whether one metric systematically outperforms the other or if the gap varies.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "row_comparison",
      "trend"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h4",
    "question": "Which dataset shows the highest overall r2 for any global metric, and what is that value?",
    "rationale": "Identify the dataset that is best explained by the global metrics, indicating ease of prediction.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "column_extrema",
      "row_extrema"
    ]
  }
]

#15 · record_id=191 · paper_id=1804.02472v1
Neural models of factuality
Missing: 4raw 4verified 4source base

Caption

Table 9: MAE of L-biLSTM(2)-S and L-biLSTM(2)-S+lexfeats, for predictions on events in UDS-IH2-dev that are xcomp-governed by an infinitival-taking verb.

Gold Description

Despite the underperformance of these features overall, Table 9 shows that they may still improve performance in the subset of instances where they appear.

High-level questions

  {
    "id": "h1",
    "question": "Which verb has the highest MAE for each model?",

#16 · record_id=196 · paper_id=1704.04455v2
Cardinal Virtues: Extracting Relation Cardinalities from Text
Covered: 1Partially covered: 2Missing: 1raw 4verified 4source base

Caption

Table 1: NE-tags of numbers in Wikipedia.

Gold Description

The distribution of their named-entity (NE) tags, according to Stanford NE-tagger, is shown in Table 1. While temporal-related numbers are the most frequent, around 40% are classified only as unspecific NUMBER. By manually checking 100 random NUMBERs, we observed that 47 are relation cardinalities,1 i.e., approximately 18.86% of all numbers in Wikipedia are relation cardinalities.

High-level questions
[
  {
    "id": "h1",
    "question": "Which NE tag for numbers occurs most frequently in Wikipedia?",
    "rationale": "Identify the maximum frequency among NE tags to understand the most common type of number mention.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "row_extrema"
    ]
  },

    "id": "h2",
    "question": "Which NE tag occurs least frequently?",

    ]

]

#17 · record_id=213 · paper_id=1904.02338v2
Consistency by Agreement in Zero-shot Neural Machine Translation
Partially covered: 1Missing: 3raw 4verified 4source base

Caption

Table 5: Results on our proposed IWSLT17?

Gold Description

on our proposed preprocessed IWSLT17(cid:63) that eliminates the overlap and reduces the number of supervised directions (8), there is a considerable gap between the supervised and zeroshot performance of Basic. Agree performs better than Basic and is slightly worse than Pivot.

High-level questions

#18 · record_id=231 · paper_id=1707.05853v2
Encoding Word Confusion Networks with Recurrent Neural Networksfor Dialog State Tracking
Covered: 1Partially covered: 1Missing: 2raw 4verified 4source base

Caption

Table 2: DSTC2 test set accuracy for 1-best ASR outputs of ten runs with different random seeds in the format average maximumminimum.

Gold Description

As can be seen from Table 2, the DST accuracy slightly increases for the higher-quality live ASR outputs. More importantly, the DST performance drastically increases, when we evaluate on the manual transcripts that reflect the true user utterances nearly perfectly.

High-level questions

    "id": "h1",

#19 · record_id=234 · paper_id=1805.03871v1
Obligation and Prohibition Extraction Using Hierarchical RNNs
Partially covered: 1Missing: 3raw 4verified 4source base

Caption

Table 3: Precision, recall, f1, and auc scores, with the best results in bold and gray background.

Gold Description

Table 3 reports the precision, recall, F1 score, area under the precision-recall curve (AUC) per class, as well as micro- and macro-averages. The self-attention mechanism (BILSTM-ATT) leads to clear overall improvements (in macro and micro F1 and AUC, Table 3) comparing to the plain BILSTM, supporting the hypothesis that selfattention allows the classifier to focus on indicative tokens. Allowing the BILSTM to consider tokens of neighboring sentences (X-BILSTM-ATT) does not lead to any clear overall improvements. The hierarchical H-BILSTM-ATT clearly outperforms the other three methods, supporting the hypothesis that considering entire sections and allowing the sentence embeddings to interact in the upper BILSTM (Fig. 3) is beneficial.

High-level questions
[
  {
    "id": "h1",
    "question": "Which model variant achieves the highest macro-average F1 score?",
    "rationale": "The macro-average row aggregates performance across all gold classes, so identifying the best model on this metric indicates overall effectiveness.",

      "row_extrema"
    ]
  },

    "question": "For the 'Prohibition' gold class, which model has the highest recall?",

      "row_extrema"
    ]
  },

#20 · record_id=241 · paper_id=1805.03871v1
Obligation and Prohibition Extraction Using Hierarchical RNNs
Covered: 1Partially covered: 1Missing: 3raw 5verified 5source base

Caption

Table 4: Training times and parameters to learn.

Gold Description

H-BILSTM-ATT is also much faster to train than BILSTM and BILSTM-ATT (Table 4), even though it has more parameters, because it converges faster (5-7 epochs vs. 12-15).

#21 · record_id=253 · paper_id=1906.06947v1
Open Domain Event Extraction Using Neural Latent Variable Models
Covered: 1Partially covered: 1Missing: 2raw 4verified 4source base

Caption

Table 5: Averaged slot coherence results.

Gold Description

Table 5 shows the comparison of averaged slot coherence results over all the slots in the schemas. The averaged slot coherence of ODEE-FER is the highest, The averaged slot coherence of ODEE-F is comparable to that of Nguyen et al. (2015) (p = 0.3415), The scores of ODEE-FE (p = 0.06) and ODEE-FER (p = 10−5) are both higher than that of ODEE-F,

#22 · record_id=292 · paper_id=1807.04053v1
UniParse: A universal graph-based parsing toolkit
Partially covered: 2Missing: 2raw 4verified 4source base

Caption

Figure 1, Listing 1 & Table 1: (Right code snippet) Implementation of Kiperwasser and Goldberg (2016)’s neural parser in only a few lines using UniParse. (Right table and left figure) Seconds a decoder takes to decode an entire dataset, given a set of scores. Score matrix entries are generated uniformly on [0, 1]. The random generated data has an impact on CLE since worst-case performance depends on the sorting bottleneck; the figure demonstrates this by the increasingly broad standard deviation band. Experiments are run on an Ubuntu machine with an Intel Xeon E5-2660, 2.60GHz CPU.

Gold Description

Seconds a decoder takes to decode an entire dataset, given a set of scores. Score matrix entries are We compare Cython implementations in Table 1 and Figure 1 over randomised score input. Note that our implementations are significantly faster.

High-level questions
[
  {
    "id": "h1",
    "question": "Which algorithm-dataset pair achieves the minimum and maximum decoding time?",
    "rationale": "Identify the fastest and slowest configurations.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "column_extrema",
      "row_extrema"
    ]
  },

    "id": "h2",

    "suggested_operations": [
      "row_comparison"
    ]
  },

    "suggested_operations": [
      "column_correlation"
    ]
  },

    "id": "h4",
    "question": "How many algorithms achieve a throughput of more than 1000 sentences per second?",

    "suggested_operations": [

    ]
  }
]

#23 · record_id=312 · paper_id=2002.10345v1
Improving BERT Fine-Tuning via Self-Ensemble and Self-Distillation
Missing: 3raw 3verified 3source base

Caption

Table 2: Results on IMDb dataset over different teacher sizes. BERTSDV(K=1) is same as BERTSDA(K=1). For IMDb and AG’s News, we report test error rate (%). For SNLI, we report accuracy (%). T denotes the total number of iterations.

Gold Description

We choose different teacher size K and evaluate our models in three datasets. Table 2 shows that teacher size is sensitive to datasets. Therefore, we select the best teacher size for each dataset in the following experiment.

High-level questions
[
  {
    "id": "h1",
    "question": "For IMDb, which model and K value achieve the lowest error rate, and how does it compare to the highest error rate?",
    "rationale": "Identify the best and worst performing configurations on IMDb to understand the range of performance.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "column_extrema",
      "row_comparison"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h2",
    "question": "As K increases from 1 to 5, what is the trend in AG's News error rate for BERTSDV and BERTSDA?",
    "rationale": "Analyze the effect of teacher size on AG's News performance to see if larger teachers consistently improve or degrade results.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "trend",
      "row_comparison"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h3",
    "question": "For SNLI accuracy, which K value yields the best performance for each model, and are there any ties?",
    "rationale": "Determine the optimal teacher size for SNLI and note if multiple K values give the same accuracy.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "column_extrema",
      "row_comparison"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h4",
    "question": "How does the performance of BERTSDA (K=1) compare to BERTSDV (K=1) across all three datasets?",
    "rationale": "Compare the two models at the same teacher size to see which is generally better.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "row_comparison"
    ]
  }
]

#24 · record_id=315 · paper_id=1912.07491v1
Improving Knowledge-aware Dialogue Generation via Knowledge Base Question Answering
Covered: 1Partially covered: 1Missing: 1raw 4verified 3source base

Caption

Table 2: Automatic evaluation with perplexity.

Gold Description

As shown in Table 2, TransDG achieves the lowest perplexity on all the datasets, indicating that the generated responses are more grammatical.

High-level questions
[
  {
    "id": "h1",
    "question": "Which model achieves the lowest overall perplexity?",
    "rationale": "Overall perplexity is a key metric for dialogue generation quality; the model with the lowest value is the best performing in terms of perplexity.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "column_extrema"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h2",
    "question": "Which models have perplexity below 40 in the High category?",
    "rationale": "The High category likely corresponds to high-complexity instances; models with low perplexity here demonstrate strong handling of difficult cases.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "threshold_count"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h3",
    "question": "How does TransDG compare to CopyNet across all categories?",
    "rationale": "Both are strong baselines; comparing their perplexity values across Overall, High, Medium, Low, and OOV reveals their relative strengths and weaknesses.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "row_comparison"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h4",
    "question": "Is there a consistent increasing trend in perplexity from High to Medium to Low categories across models?",
    "rationale": "The categories represent different difficulty levels; a trend of increasing perplexity would indicate that models find Low (least context) more challenging than High (most context).",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "trend"
    ]
  }
]

#25 · record_id=317 · paper_id=1912.07491v1
Improving Knowledge-aware Dialogue Generation via Knowledge Base Question Answering
Partially covered: 1Missing: 3raw 4verified 4source base

Caption

Table 4: Automatic evaluation with BLEU.

Gold Description

The BLEU values shown in Table 4 demonstrates the comparison results from word-level overlaps. TransDG tends to generate responses that are more similar to the gold responses than baselines in most cases.

High-level questions
[
  {
    "id": "h1",
    "question": "Which model achieves the highest BLEU-1 score?",
    "rationale": "BLEU-1 is the most commonly reported metric; identifying the top performer provides insight into overall generation quality.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "column_extrema"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h2",
    "question": "How does TransDG compare to CopyNet across all BLEU n-grams?",
    "rationale": "TransDG appears top on most n-grams except BLEU-2; a detailed comparison reveals consistency or trade-offs.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "row_comparison",
      "trend"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h3",
    "question": "What is the performance gap between the best and worst model for each BLEU metric?",
    "rationale": "The gap quantifies the improvement possible over the baseline (Seq2Seq) and helps judge practical significance.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "column_extrema",
      "row_comparison"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h4",
    "question": "Do BLEU scores decrease monotonically as n increases for each model?",
    "rationale": "Expected behavior is a decrease; checking monotonicity validates the consistency of metric behavior across models.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "trend"
    ]
  }
]

#26 · record_id=319 · paper_id=1912.07491v1
Improving Knowledge-aware Dialogue Generation via Knowledge Base Question Answering
Partially covered: 2Missing: 3raw 5verified 5source base

Caption

Table 7: Ablation results of TransDG on the test set. Here, Entity represents entity score.

Gold Description

The ablation test results are reported in Table 7. From the results, we can observe that the performance of TransDG drops sharply when we discard the question representation module and the knowledge selection module transferred from KBQA. Response guiding attention also has noticeable impact on the performance of TransDG, especially on BLEU scores. In addition, the second-step decoder can improve the ability of TransDG to generate relevant entities per response.

High-level questions
[
  {
    "id": "h1",
    "question": "Which model achieves the lowest perplexity, and how much does perplexity increase when key components are removed?",
    "rationale": "Identifying the best perplexity and the degradation from ablations helps assess each component's contribution to language model performance.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "column_extrema",
      "row_comparison"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h2",
    "question": "What is the impact of removing the Query Refinement component (w/o QRT) on Entity score compared to the full model and other single-component removals?",
    "rationale": "Entity score measures knowledge integration; comparing w/o QRT with others highlights its specific role.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "column_extrema",
      "row_comparison"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h3",
    "question": "Among single-component removals, which one causes the largest drop in BLEU-1 relative to TransDG, and by how much?",
    "rationale": "BLEU-1 reflects dialogue quality; finding the most harmful removal guides focused improvement.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "column_extrema",
      "row_comparison"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h4",
    "question": "Is there a trend of increasing perplexity from the full model to single removals to double removal (w/o QRT+KST)?",
    "rationale": "This examines whether performance degrades monotonically with more component removal, indicating additive or interactive effects.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "trend",
      "row_comparison"
    ]
  }
]

#27 · record_id=320 · paper_id=1909.12764v1
Improving Semantic Parsing with Neural Generator-Reranker Architecture
Covered: 1Partially covered: 2Missing: 2raw 5verified 5source base

Caption

Table 3: Test accuracy for all models on OVERNIGHT dataset, which has eight domains: Basketball, Blocks, Calendar, Housing, Publications, Recipes, Restaurants, and Social. We use the generator-reranker (G-R) architecture with different options. Beam-n: Beam search is applied with size n, pQ: The critic is pre-trained over the Quora dataset, TH1: rerank if there is at least one score above 0.5, TH2: rerank if best score−second best score>0.001. The candidate logical forms are processed with templated expansions method (Section 3.2.3) in this experiment.

Gold Description

We compare our model with the state-of-the-art models in Table 3. As Table 3 shows, this model alone (without any reranking) improves the state-of-the-art performance from 79.6% to 82.15% accuracy Increasing the beam size improves the performance as expected. Using a pre-trained model improves the performance as well. Reranking with a threshold rule may be helpful for the overall architecture. We observe that reranking by the critic at all times may not be the best approach. We note that choosing not to rerank when all scores are below 0.5 increases the performance further. On the other hand, reranking if the difference between the best score and second best score is above the threshold we set does not help in this case. The overall architecture improves the performance of the generator (82.1% accuracy) to 83.7% accuracy.

#28 · record_id=321 · paper_id=2003.03716v1
Investigating the Decoders of Maximum Likelihood Sequence Models: A Look-ahead Approach
Partially covered: 1Missing: 3raw 4verified 4source base

Caption

Table 1: The performances of the IM2LATEX-100K Bi-LSTM model. We discover that the look-ahead improves the model from the greedy search method — noted that LA is more directly comparable to the greedy search because of their same beam size. We also show the scores of the beam search for the reference

Gold Description

Table 1: The performances of the IM2LATEX-100K Bi-LSTM model. We discover that the look-ahead improves the model from the greedy search method — noted that LA is more directly comparable to the greedy search because of their same beam size. We also show the scores of the beam search for the reference

#29 · record_id=335 · paper_id=2002.00293v1
Beat the AI: Investigating Adversarial Human Annotationsfor Reading Comprehension
Missing: 4raw 4verified 4source base

Caption

Table 5: Training models on various datasets, each with 10,000 samples, and measuring their generalisation to different evaluation datasets. Results in bold indicate the best result per model.

Gold Description

We next conduct a series of experiments in which we train on DBiDAF, DBERT, and DRoBERTa, and observe how well models can then learn to generalise on the respective test portions of these datasets. Table 5 shows the results, and there is a multitude of observations. First, one clear trend we observe across all training data setups is a clear negative performance progression when evaluated against datasets constructed with a stronger model in the loop. This trend holds true for all but the BiDAF model, in each of the training configurations, and for each of the evaluation datasets. For example, RoBERTa trained on DRoBERTa achieves 71.4, 53.5, 48.6 and 38.9 F1 when evaluated on DSQuAD, DBiDAF, DBERT and DRoBERTa, respectively. Second, we observe that the BiDAF model is not able to generalise well to datasets constructed with a model in the loop, independent of its training setup. In particular it is unable to learn from DBiDAF, thus failing to overcome some of its own blind spots through adversarial training. Both when training only on DBiDAF, as well as when adding DSQuAD to DBiDAF during training (cf. Table 6), BiDAF performs poorly across all the adversarial datasets. results in Table 5, where training on DBiDAF in several cases led to better generalisation than training on DRoBERTa. we further train each of our three models on either DBiDAF, DBERT, or DRoBERTa and test on DSQuAD, with results in the DSQuAD columns of Table 5. First, we observe clear generalisation improvements towards DDROP across all models compared to training on DSQuAD(10K) when using any of the DBiDAF, DBERT, or DRoBERTa datasets for training. That is, including a model in the loop for the training dataset leads to improved transfer towards DDROP. Note that the DROP dataset also makes use of a BiDAF model in the loop during annotation; these results are in line with our prior observations when testing the same setups on DBiDAF, DBERT and DRoBERTa, compared to training on DSQuAD(10K). Second, we observe overall strong transfer results towards DNQ: up to 71.0F1 for a BERT model trained on DBiDAF. Note that this result is similar and even slightly improves over model training with SQuAD data of the same size. That is, relative to training on SQuAD data, training on adversarially collected data DBiDAF does not impede generalisation to the DNQ dataset, which was created without a model in the annotation loop. We then however see a similar negative performance progression as observed before when testing on DSQuAD: the stronger the model in the annotation loop of the training dataset, the lower the test accuracy on test data from a data distribution composed without using a model in the loop.

High-level questions
[
  {
    "id": "h1",
    "question": "Which model (row) achieves the highest Exact Match (EM) on the DSQuAD test set, and what is that value?",
    "rationale": "Identifying the best-performing model on the DSQuAD dataset helps assess which architecture or training approach yields the strongest generalization to this specific domain, highlighting potential top candidates for further analysis.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "column_extrema"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h2",
    "question": "For models trained on 10,000 samples (rows where 'Trained On' = 10.0), what is the average F1 score across all evaluation datasets?",
    "rationale": "Comparing the average generalization performance of models that were explicitly trained on a fixed sample size reveals whether training on a specific dataset (e.g., DSQuAD) leads to broader or narrower generalization across test sets.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "row_comparison"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h3",
    "question": "Is there a positive correlation between the F1 scores on DBiDAF and DBERT across all models?",
    "rationale": "Assessing the correlation between performances on two different evaluation datasets can indicate whether models that perform well on one also tend to perform well on the other, suggesting shared underlying difficulty or model capability.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "column_correlation"
    ]
  },

#30 · record_id=351 · paper_id=1910.02655v1
BERT for Evidence Retrieval and Claim Verification
Partially covered: 1Missing: 3raw 4verified 4source base

Caption

Table 2: Development set verification scores.

Gold Description

In Table 2, we compare the development set results of the state of the art methods with the BERT model trained on different retrieved evidence sets. The BERT claim verification system even if it is trained on the UKP-Athene sentence retrieval component (Hanselowski et al., 2018), the state of the art method with the highest recall, improves both label accuracy and FEVER score. Training based on the BERT sentence retrieval predictions significantly enhances the verification results because while it explicitly improves the FEVER score by providing more correct evidence sentences, it provides a better training set for the verification system. The large BERTs are only trained on the best retrieval systems, and as expected significantly improve the performance.

#31 · record_id=352 · paper_id=1910.14539v1
Naver Labs Europe’s Systems for theDocument-Level Generation and Translation Task at WNGT 2019
Missing: 4raw 4verified 4source base

Caption

Table 3: Doc-level BLEU scores on the DGT valid and test sets of our submitted models in all tracks.

Gold Description

For each track, we selected the best models according to their BLEU score on DGT-valid. The scores are shown in Table 3, We see that in the same data conditions (unconstrained mode), the MT+NLG models are not better than the pure MT models.

High-level questions
[
  {
    "id": "h1",
    "question": "Which model achieves the highest BLEU score on the test set, and what is that score?",
    "rationale": "Identifying the best performing model helps benchmark progress and guide further improvements.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "row_extrema"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h2",
    "question": "For the EN target, how does the performance of constrained MT (yes) compare to unconstrained MT (no) on the valid set?",
    "rationale": "Understanding the impact of constraints on MT quality is crucial for resource allocation.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "row_comparison"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h3",
    "question": "Is there a consistent drop in BLEU scores from the valid set to the test set across all systems?",
    "rationale": "Checking for overfitting or domain shift by comparing validation and test performance.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "trend"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h4",
    "question": "What is the difference in test BLEU scores between the best EN system and the best DE system?",
    "rationale": "Quantifying language-specific performance gaps can inform multilingual model development.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "row_comparison"
    ]
  }
]

Partially covered: 3Missing: 1raw 4verified 4source base

Caption

Table 7: English NLG ablation study, starting from a 3 best player baseline (the submitted NLG model has 4 players). BLEU averages over 3 runs. Standard deviation ranges between 0.1 and 0.4.

Gold Description

From Table 7, we see that sorting players helps, but only slightly. Using only team-level information, and no information about players gives worse but still decent BLEU scores. Week day, player position or team-level aggregated scores can be removed without hurting BLEU. However, information about next games seems useful. Interestingly, relying on position only and removing most tags (e.g., , ) seems to be fine. In this case, we also print all-zero stats, for the position of each statistic to be consistent across players and games.

#33 · record_id=360 · paper_id=1906.04106v1
Neural Keyphrase Generation via Reinforcement Learning with Adaptive Rewards
Missing: 3raw 3verified 3source retry

Caption

Table 6: Keyphrase prediction results on the KP20k dataset with our new evaluation method.

Gold Description

Table 6 shows that for all generative models, the evaluation scores computed by our method are higher than those computed by prior method.

#34 · record_id=362 · paper_id=2002.04250v2
Non-Autoregressive Neural Dialogue Generation
Covered: 1Partially covered: 1Missing: 1raw 3verified 3source retry

Caption

Table 3: Human judgments for Coherence and Content Richeness of the different models.

Gold Description

For dialogue coherence trend is 3. that NonAR+MMI than AR+MMI, followed by AR and Non-AR. AR is slightly better than Non-AR. For Content Richness, the is significantly better proposed NonAR+MMI than AR+MMI, and the gap is greater than dialogue coherence. The output from the AR+MMI model is thus by far less diverse than nonAR+MMI, which obtains the MMI score for each generated token.

  {
    "id": "h1",
    "question": "Which model achieves the highest agreement percentage (agr%) for Coherence?",
    "rationale": "Identifies the best-performing model in terms of coherence as judged by humans.",

      "column_extrema"
    ]
  },

#35 · record_id=363 · paper_id=2002.04250v2
Non-Autoregressive Neural Dialogue Generation
Partially covered: 3Missing: 1raw 4verified 4source retry

Caption

Table 4: The performances of NonAR+MMI methods on WMT14 En↔De and WMT16 Ro→En. Results from Gu et al. (2018); Lee et al. (2018); Ma et al. (2019) are copied from original papers for reference purposes.

Gold Description

Results are shown in Table 4. As can be seen, the incorporation of MMI model significantly improves MT performances.

#36 · record_id=366 · paper_id=1911.01055v1
On the Effectiveness of the Pooling Methods for Biomedical Relation Extraction with Deep Learning
Missing: 3Contradicted: 1raw 4verified 4source retry

Caption

Table 1: Results on DDI 2013

Gold Description

1. Comparing ENT-SENT, ENT-DYM and ENT-ONLY, we see that the pooling methods over the whole sentence (i.e., ENT-SENT and ENT-DYM) are significantly better than ENT-ONLY that only focuses on the two entity mentions of interest in the DDI-2013 dataset. This is true across different deep learning models in this work. Comparing ENT-SENT and ENT-DYM, their performance are comparable in DDI-2013 (except for CNN where ENT-DYM is better). Comparing the syntax-based pooling methods and the non-syntax pooling methods, the pooling based on dependency paths (i.e., ENT-DEP0) is worse than the non-syntax pooling methods (i.e., ENT-SENT and ENT-DYM) and perform comparably with ENT-ONLY in the DDI-2013 dataset over all the models (except for the CNN model where ENT-ONLY is much worse).

High-level questions
[
  {
    "id": "h1",
    "question": "Which pooling method achieves the highest F1 score across all runs, and what is that score?",
    "rationale": "Identifies the best overall performer to guide insight on effective pooling for biomedical relation extraction.",

      "column_extrema",
      "row_extrema"
    ]
  },

    "id": "h2",
    "question": "Which model exhibits the largest gap between precision and recall, and is it consistent across runs?",

    "suggested_operations": [
      "row_comparison",
      "column_correlation",
      "trend"
    ]
  },

    "question": "How does the ENT-ONLY method compare to methods incorporating syntactic information (SENT, DYM, DEP0, DEP1) in terms of F1?",

    "suggested_operations": [
      "row_comparison",
      "trend"
    ]

    ]

]

#37 · record_id=367 · paper_id=1905.07588v1
BERTSel: Answer Selection with Pre-trained Models
Partially covered: 1Missing: 3raw 4verified 4source retry

Caption

Table 2: Results of BERTbase in test set of five datasets with different epochs. The SOTA results are from Madabushi et al. (2018) (TrecQA), Sha et al. (2018) (WikiQA, SemEvalcQA-16), Tay et al. (2018b) (YahooQA), Nakov et al. (2017) (SemEvalcQA-17).

Gold Description

We show the main result in Table 2 and 3. Despite training on a fraction of the data available, the proposed BERT-based models surpass the previous state-of-the-art models by a large margin on all datasets

High-level questions
[
  {
    "id": "h1",
    "question": "For which datasets does increasing epochs from 3 to 5 yield the largest improvement in MRR and MAP?",
    "rationale": "Comparing epoch=3 and epoch=5 values for each dataset identifies where additional training provides the most benefit.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "row_comparison",
      "column_extrema"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h2",
    "question": "On which datasets does BERTbase at epoch=5 outperform the reported SOTA, and by how much?",
    "rationale": "Comparing BERTbase epoch=5 to SOTA values (excluding missing SOTA for YahooQA MRR) highlights competitive advantages.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "row_comparison",
      "threshold_count"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h3",
    "question": "What is the range (max-min) of MRR values across datasets for epoch=5?",
    "rationale": "Calculating the highest and lowest MRR among all datasets for epoch=5 shows variability in performance.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "column_extrema"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h4",
    "question": "Is there a consistent relationship between MRR and MAP across all datasets and epochs?",
    "rationale": "Examining whether datasets with high MRR also have high MAP indicates metric consistency.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "column_correlation"
    ]
  }
]

#38 · record_id=368 · paper_id=1911.01055v1
On the Effectiveness of the Pooling Methods for Biomedical Relation Extraction with Deep Learning
Missing: 4raw 4verified 4source retry

Caption

Table 2: Results on BioNLP BB3

Gold Description

However, this comparison is reversed for the BB3 dataset where ENT-ONLY is in general better or comparable to ENT-SENT and ENT-DYM over different deep learning models. in the BB3 dataset, ENT-SENT singificantly outperforms ENT-DYM over all the models. When we switch to the BB3 dataset, it turns out thatENT-DEP0 is significantly better than all the non-syntax pooling methods (i.e., ENT-ONLY, ENT-SENT and ENT-DYM) for all the comparing models.

High-level questions

    "id": "h1",

      "row_comparison"
    ]
  },

#39 · record_id=380 · paper_id=1904.04745v1
Cross-Modal Self-Attention Network for Referring Image Segmentation
Partially covered: 1Missing: 3raw 4verified 4source retry

Caption

Table 3: Ablation study on the UNC val set. The top four methods compare results of different methods for multimodal feature representations. The bottom five results show comparisons of multi-level feature fusion methods. CMSA and GF denote the proposed cross-modal self-attention and gated multi-level fusion modules. All methods use the same base model (DeepLab-101) and DenseCRF for postprocessing. ∗The numbers for [15] are slightly higher than original numbers reported in their paper which did not use DenseCRF postprocessing.

Gold Description

As shown in Table 3 (top 4 rows), the proposed crossmodal self-attentive feature based approaches achieve significantly better performance than other baselines. the word based method CMSA-W outperforms sentence based method CMSA-S for multimodal feature representation. As presented in the bottom 5 rows in Table 3, the proposed gated multi-level fusion outperforms these other multi-scale feature fusion methods.

High-level questions
[
  {
    "id": "h1",
    "question": "Which method achieves the highest IoU, and what is the IoU value?",

    ]
  },

    ]

#40 · record_id=402 · paper_id=1810.02229v1
Italian Event Detection Goes Deep Learning
Missing: 4raw 4verified 4source retry

Caption

Table 1: Distribution of the event mentions per POS per token in all datasets of the EVENTI corpus.

Gold Description

Tables 1 and 2 report, respectively, the distribution of the events per token part-of speech (POS) and per event class. verbs are the largest annotated category, followed by nouns, adjectives, and prepositional phrases.

High-level questions
[
  {
    "id": "h1",
    "question": "Which POS category has the highest number of event mentions in each dataset?",
    "rationale": "Identifying the dominant POS helps understand the primary sources of event mentions.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "row_extrema"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h2",
    "question": "What is the ratio of verb event mentions to overall event tokens in each dataset?",
    "rationale": "Verbs are a key indicator of events; their proportion shows linguistic emphasis.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "row_comparison"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h3",
    "question": "Are there any POS categories with fewer than 100 event mentions in any dataset?",
    "rationale": "Threshold analysis reveals rare or potentially unreliable POS categories.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "threshold_count"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h4",
    "question": "Is there a strong correlation between the number of event mentions in training and test sets across POS categories?",
    "rationale": "Consistency between training and test distribution is crucial for model generalization.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "column_correlation"
    ]
  }
]

#41 · record_id=415 · paper_id=1805.06061v1
SoPa: Bridging CNNs, RNNs, and Weighted Finite-State Machines
Partially covered: 2Missing: 1Contradicted: 1raw 4verified 4source retry

Caption

Table 1: Test classification accuracy (and the number of parameters used). The bottom part shows our ablation results: SoPa: our full model. SoPams1: running with max-sum semiring (rather than max-product), with the identity function as our encoder E (see Equation 3). sl: self-loops, ϵ: ϵ transitions. The final row is equivalent to a one-layer CNN.

Gold Description

Table 1 shows our main experimental results. In two of the cases (SST and ROC), SoPa outperforms all models. On Amazon, SoPa performs within 0.3 points of CNN and BiLSTM, and outperforms the other two baselines. The table also shows the number of parameters used by each model for each task. SoPa performs better or roughly the same as a BiLSTM, which has 3–6 times as many parameters. Table 1 also shows an ablation of the differences between SoPa and CNN: max-product semiring with sigmoid vs. max-sum semiring with identity, self-loops, and (cid:15)-transitions. The last line is equivalent to a CNN with multiple window sizes. Interestingly, the most notable difference between SoPa and CNN is the semiring and encoder function, while (cid:15) transitions and self-loops have little effect on performance.1

#42 · record_id=417 · paper_id=1901.01010v2
A Joint Model for Multimodal Document Quality Assessment
Covered: 2Missing: 1raw 3verified 3source retry

Caption

Table 3: Experimental results. The best result for each dataset is indicated in bold, and marked with “†” if it is significantly higher than the second best result (based on a one-tailed Wilcoxon signed-rank test; p<0.05). The results of Benchmark on the arXiv dataset are from the original paper, where the standard deviation values were not reported. All neural models except for Inceptionfixed have larger standard deviation values on arXiv than Wikipedia, which can be explained by the small size of the arXiv test set.

Gold Description

Experimental Results Table 3 shows the performance of the different models over our two datasets, in the form of the average accuracy on the test set (along with the standard deviation) over 10 runs, with different random initializations. On Wikipedia, we observe that the performance of BILSTM, INCEPTION, and JOINT is much better than that of all four baselines. INCEPTION achieves 2.9% higher accuracy than BILSTM. The performance of JOINT achieves an accuracy of 59.4%, which is 5.3% higher than using textual features alone (BILSTM) and 2.4% higher than using visual features alone (INCEPTION). Based on a one-tailed Wilcoxon signed-rank test, the performance of Jis sta of-the-art results in combination. For arXiv, baseline methods MAJORITY, BENCHMARK, and INCEPTIONFIXED outperform BILSTM over cs.ai, in large part because of the class imbalance in this dataset (90% of papers are rejected). Surprisingly, INCEPTIONFIXED is better than MAJORITY and BENCHMARK over the arXiv cs.lg subset, which verifies the usefulness of visual features, even when only the last layer is fine-tuned. Table 3 also shows that INCEPTION and BILSTM achieve similar performance on arXiv, showing that textual and visual representations are equally discriminative: INCEPTION and BILSTM are indistinguishable over cs.cl; BILSTM achieves 1.8% higher accuracy over cs.lg, while INCEPTION achieves 1.3% higher accuracy over cs.ai. Once again, the JOINT model achieves the highest accuracy on cs.ai and cs.cl by combining textual and visual representations (at a level of statistical significance for cs.ai). This, again, confirms that textual and visual state-of-the-art results. On arXiv cs.lg, JOINT achieves a 0.6% higher accuracy than INCEPTION by combining visual features and textual features, but BILSTM achieves the highest accuracy. One characteristic of cs.lg documents is

    "id": "h1",
    "question": "Which model achieves the highest accuracy on each arXiv dataset?",
    "rationale": "Identify the top-performing model per dataset to assess model effectiveness across different domains.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "row_extrema"
    ]

    "id": "h2",

      "row_comparison"

    "question": "Which model has the highest minimum accuracy across datasets?",

      "threshold_count"
    ]
  }
]

#43 · record_id=421 · paper_id=1912.00871v1
Solving Arithmetic Word Problems Automatically Using Transformer and Unambiguous Representations
Missing: 5raw 5verified 5source retry

Caption

TABLE II: Summary of BLEU scores from Table I.

Gold Description

Table III provides detailed results of Experiment 2. The numbers are absolute accuracies, i.e., they correspond to cases where the arithmetic expression generated is 100% correct, leading to the correct numeric answer. Results by , , , are sparse but indicate the scale of success compared to recent past approaches. Prefix, postfix, and infix representations in Table III show that network capabilities are changed by how teachable the target data is. While our networks fell short of AI2 testing accuracy, we present state-of-the-art results for the remaining three datasets. The type 2 postfix Transformer received the highest testing average of 87.2%. Our attempt at language pre-training fell short of our expectations in all but one tested dataset.

#44 · record_id=427 · paper_id=1910.12647v1
HUBERT Untangles BERT to Improve Transfer across NLP Tasks
Missing: 3raw 4verified 3source retry

Caption

Table 5: Test set results for HUBERT (Transformer) and BERT. BERT accuracy indicates test results on target corpus (without transfer) for bert-base-uncased which are directly taken from the GLUE leaderboard. Fine-tuned accuracy are the test results for best performing HUBERT (Transformer) model on target dev set after transfer (see Tables 3 and 4).

Gold Description

We also verified that our TPR layer is not hurting the performance by comparing the test set results for HUBERT (Transformer) and BERT. The results are obtained by submitting models to the GLUE evaluation server. The results are presented in Table 5.

High-level questions
[
  {
    "id": "h1",
    "question": "On which target corpus does HUBERT achieve the highest accuracy improvement over BERT, and what is the improvement?",

      "row_comparison"

  }

#45 · record_id=441 · paper_id=1906.06442v1
Tagged Back-Translation
Missing: 3raw 4verified 3source retry

Caption

Table 5: Results on WMT15 EnFr, with bitext, BT, NoisedBT, and TaggedBT.

Gold Description

We performed a minimal set of experiments on WMT EnFr, which are summarized in Table 5. In this case, we see that BT alone hurts performance compared to the strong bitext baseline, but NoisedBT indeed surpasses the bitext model. It is worth noting that our numbers are lower than those reported by Edunov et al. (2018) on the years they report (36.1, 43.8, and 40.9 on 2013, 2014, and 2015 respectively). On WMT16 EnRo, TaggedBT improves on vanilla BT by 0.4 BLEU.

High-level questions
[
  {
    "id": "h1",
    "question": "Which model achieves the highest overall average BLEU score, and what is its value?",
    "rationale": "Identifying the best-performing model based on the average score across all years is a primary analytical goal for comparing methods.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "row_extrema"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h2",
    "question": "How does the performance of TaggedBT compare to that of NoisedBT across each year?",
    "rationale": "Since both are back-translation variants, a year-by-year comparison reveals which method is consistently better and the magnitude of differences.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "row_comparison"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h3",
    "question": "Which year exhibits the largest performance gap between the best and worst model?",
    "rationale": "Identifying the year with the largest spread helps understand which conditions most differentiate model quality.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "column_extrema",
      "row_comparison"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h4",
    "question": "Is there a general upward trend in BLEU scores over the years for any model?",
    "rationale": "Observing trends across years (2008-2015) indicates whether models improve over time, possibly due to more training data or other factors.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "trend"
    ]
  }
]

#46 · record_id=453 · paper_id=2003.08875v1
Beheshti-NER: Persian named entity recognition Using BERT
Missing: 4raw 5verified 4source retry

Caption

Table 4: Phrase-level evaluation for subtask A: 3-classes

Gold Description

Tables 4, 5, 6, 7 and 8 show the results of evaluation reported by competition for all teams which participated in the challenge. Our method is mentioned as Beheshti-NER-1 Table 4 and 5 show the results for subtask A. according to the tables, we reached to 84.0% and 87.9% F1 score respectively for phrase and word level evaluations.

High-level questions
[
  {
    "id": "h1",
    "question": "Which team achieves the highest F1 score on the In Domain test data?",
    "rationale": "Identifying the best-performing team on in-domain data helps assess model robustness on familiar data.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "column_extrema"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h2",
    "question": "How does the total F1 score vary across all teams, and which team has the lowest total F1?",
    "rationale": "Comparing overall F1 scores reveals the range of model performance and the worst-performing team.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "column_extrema",
      "row_comparison"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h3",
    "question": "Is there a consistent performance drop in F1 from In Domain to Out Domain across teams?",
    "rationale": "A consistent drop indicates generalization difficulty; identifying exceptions may reveal robustness.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "trend",
      "row_comparison"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h4",
    "question": "Which team exhibits the largest gap between precision and recall on Out Domain data?",
    "rationale": "A large precision-recall gap may indicate imbalance in model predictions.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "column_extrema",
      "row_comparison"
    ]
  }
]

#47 · record_id=458 · paper_id=1911.10392
When is ACL’s Deadline? A Scientific Conversational Agent
Missing: 4raw 4verified 4source retry

Caption

Table 2: The accuracy(%) of the ML models for NLU.

Gold Description

First, we represent utterances by their TF-IDF representations as feature-vectors. We then supply the vector representation of each utterance to a Support Vector Machine (SVM) with a linear kernel for domain and intent identification, and to a Hidden Markov Model (HMM) for slot filling. Second, we encode words in a dialogue utterance by GLoVe, as benchmark pre-trained word embeddings, to include the semantic relationships among words. We compute the average of word embeddings in an utterance to represent the utterance by a vector. Table 2 shows the performance of the described models.

#48 · record_id=463 · paper_id=6
Corpus-driven Thematic Hierarchy Induction
Partially covered: 1Missing: 2raw 3verified 3source retry

Caption

Table 5: Cross-lingual evaluation, global ranker

Gold Description

Table 5 contrasts the performance of THs induced from English and German training data, and evaluated on German and English test data respectively. While the cross-lingual performance is expectedly lower than the monolingual performance, it outperforms the random baseline by a large margin, suggesting the potential for crosslingual hierarchy induction.

High-level questions
[
  {
    "id": "h1",
    "question": "Which training condition achieves the highest EN-test score, and what is that score?",
    "rationale": "Identifying the maximum EN-test performance helps understand which training language generalizes best to English test data.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "column_extrema"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h2",
    "question": "Which training condition achieves the highest DE-test score, and what is that score?",
    "rationale": "Identifying the maximum DE-test performance helps understand which training language generalizes best to German test data.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "column_extrema"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h3",
    "question": "How large is the gap between the upper bound (UB) and the best single-language training model (EN-train or DE-train) on the EN-test?",
    "rationale": "Quantifying the gap between the theoretical upper bound and the best cross-lingual model reveals the potential for improvement in cross-lingual transfer.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "row_extrema",
      "row_comparison"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h4",
    "question": "How many training models achieve a score above 800 on both English and German test sets?",
    "rationale": "Counting models that pass a high threshold on both tests indicates robustness in cross-lingual performance.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "threshold_count"
    ]
  }
]

#49 · record_id=465 · paper_id=1908.11254v1
FAMULUS: Interactive Annotation and Feedback Generation for Teaching Diagnostic Reasoning
Missing: 4raw 4verified 4source retry

Caption

Table 1: Individual macro-F1 scores following Schulz et al. (2019a) for each of the epistemic activities. The BiLSTM uses FastText embeddings Bojanowski et al. (2017). This architecture is equal to Flair when only using FastText embeddings. UB reports the human upper bound (inter-annotator agreement) indicating room for improvement.

Gold Description

In Table 1, we report the performance of the BiLSTM implementation for predicting epistemic activities in the Med and TEd data. As we can see, the difficulty of predicting the classes varies between different activities. Despite some room for improvement with respect to the human upper bound (UB) based on inter-rater agreement, the interactive nature of FAMULUS helps in succeeding in this attempt by continually improving the model when new data is available. We conduct similar experiments for the prediction of fine-grained diagnostic entities, but omit a comprehensive discussion due to space limitations.

High-level questions
[
  {
    "id": "h1",
    "question": "Which epistemic activity yields the highest BiLSTM macro-F1 score, and what is that score?",
    "rationale": "Identifying the best-performing activity for the BiLSTM model helps prioritize focus areas.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "row_extrema"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h2",
    "question": "For which epistemic activity is the gap between BiLSTM and UB largest?",
    "rationale": "The largest gap indicates where the model has the most room for improvement relative to human performance.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "row_comparison"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h3",
    "question": "What is the range (max-min) of macro-F1 scores for the human upper bound (UB) across all activities?",
    "rationale": "The UB range shows variability in human agreement across activities, indicating differences in difficulty.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "column_extrema"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h4",
    "question": "How does the best BiLSTM score compare to the worst UB score?",
    "rationale": "This comparison reveals if the model's peak performance surpasses the lower bound of human agreement.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "row_comparison",
      "column_extrema"
    ]
  }
]

#50 · record_id=477 · paper_id=5
Ranking Generated Summaries by Correctness: An Interesting but Challenging Application for Natural Language Inference
Covered: 2Partially covered: 1Missing: 1raw 4verified 4source retry

Caption

Table 2: Fraction of incorrect summaries at first position after reranking with different NLI models. ↑ and ↓ show the absolute number of improved (incorrect replaced by correct) and worsened (vice versa) instances.

Gold Description

For 107 out of the 200 documents, an incorrect and correct summary is among the 5 alternatives. Table 2 shows that in this sample from the validation data, the fraction of incorrect summaries at first position, when the 5 alternatives are ranked as during beam search, is at 42.1%. Using entailment probabilities of ESIM and InferSent, we can slightly improve upon that and reduce incorrect summaries. However, with DA and SSE, more incorrect summaries end up in the first position. Note that these results are not in line with the model's NLI accuracies, underlining that performance on NLI does not directly transfer to our task. Only for BERT, which outperforms the other models on NLI by a large margin, we also see substantially better reranking performance. But even for this powerful model, more than half of the errors still remain in the summaries.5 Interestingly, we also find that for ESIM and InferSent, reranking hurts in many cases, leaving just a few cases of net improvement. Given the validation results, we then applied reranking to the CNN-DM test data followed by a post-hoc correctness evaluation as in Section 4. We used the ESIM model and reranked all 10 beam hypotheses generated by FAS.6 In contrast to the validation sample, the fraction of incorrect summaries increases from 26% to 29% (Table 2), demonstrating that the slight improvement on the validation data does not transfer to the test set.
